parentQuestion: Most postganglionic sympathetic fibers secrete norepinephrine (NE).

scaffolds.explain: Yes! Same molecule, different names. NE/noradrenaline is the main sympathetic postganglionic neurotransmitter.

scaffolds.q: Sympathetic postganglionic neurons send signals to target organs like heart and blood vessels.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! After synapsing in ganglia, postganglionic fibers travel to effector organs to execute sympathetic responses.

scaffolds.q: 'Adrenergic' means using norepinephrine or epinephrine as neurotransmitter.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Adrenergic neurons release NE (or epi). Most sympathetic postganglionic neurons are adrenergic.

scaffolds.q: Adrenergic receptors (alpha and beta) respond to NE and epinephrine.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Alpha-1, alpha-2, beta-1, beta-2, beta-3 receptors are all adrenergic - they bind NE and epinephrine.

scaffolds.q: The word 'most' is important - there are a few sympathetic exceptions that use ACh.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Most sympathetic postganglionics use NE, but sweat glands receive sympathetic fibers that use ACh (cholinergic exception).

scaffolds.q: Sympathetic activation causes NE release at the heart, blood vessels, and other targets.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! NE binding adrenergic receptors produces fight-or-flight effects: Faster heart, vasoconstriction, etc.
Yes! This is the key distinction at the postganglionic level. Sympathetic uses NE (mostly), parasympathetic uses ACh.

scaffolds.q: Most sympathetic postganglionic fibers release norepinephrine.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The majority of sympathetic postganglionics are adrenergic. Sweat glands are the main cholinergic exception.

scaffolds.q: The statement is TRUE - MOST sympathetic postganglionic fibers use NE.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The answer is TRUE. 'Most' is accurate - NE is the dominant sympathetic postganglionic transmitter, with ACh exceptions for sweat glands.
